A.notes: Simple /health endpoint with DB check, brief gRPC mention is a bonus. ~15 lines of core code. Proportional to the ask.

B.notes: Most proportional response. Minimal /health endpoint, clean Ktor extension, offers readiness check only if asked. Exactly the right size for the problem.

C.notes: Adds /health/liveness endpoint beyond what was asked, includes K8s manifest YAML, and then offers a Spring Boot Actuator alternative. Two endpoints + K8s config + alternative approach is over-scoped.

D.notes: Actuator is a reasonable framework choice but introduces dependency management, separate management port config, and mentions auto-detection of Redis/Kafka health. Moderate over-engineering.

E.notes: Builds both a gRPC health service AND a separate HTTP server AND K8s probes. Well over 50 lines of code. Two separate servers for a health check is disproportionate.

F.notes: Provides no solution at all — just asks clarifying questions, including what language the service uses (should know it's Kotlin). Didn't over-engineer because it didn't engineer anything.

G.notes: Two full options (gRPC + HTTP with Ktor), Redis health check baked into Option 2, plus K8s manifest. Over 50 lines total. Multiple approaches adds decision burden rather than simplifying.

H.notes: Most over-engineered: DB + Redis checks, liveness endpoint, coroutine dispatchers, structured logging, security filter chain config, K8s probes. A full observability pattern for a simple health check ask.

analysis: System B is the clear winner — it delivers exactly what was asked (a simple /health endpoint) in minimal code and explicitly defers deeper checks to a follow-up, demonstrating excellent proportionality judgment. System A is a close second with a clean /health plus DB check and a brief gRPC bonus mention without over-committing. System D uses Actuator which is reasonable but introduces unnecessary config surface. From System C onward, responses progressively over-engineer: C adds liveness endpoints and K8s manifests, E builds two separate servers, G offers two full alternatives with Redis checks, and H is the most bloated with coroutine dispatchers, structured logging, security config, and multi-dependency health aggregation — all for a simple health check. System F is ranked last despite not over-engineering because it fails to provide any solution at all, asking questions it should already know the answers to. The core differentiator is whether the system recognized that 'add a health check endpoint' means a simple /health returning 200, not an observability infrastructure project.
